1: The famous time poem opens with the general principle: for everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven. The Hebrew et (time) carries the weight of appointed time — the same root used for the appointed festivals (moadim of Lev 23). This is not random chronological time (chronos) but purposeful, appointed time (kairos in Greek). The poem echoes Genesis 1's creation of ordered time and reflects the ANE view that God governs the appointment of events.
2: A time to be born and a time to die — the first pair anchors all the others. The border-events of human existence are given, not chosen. In the ancient Near East, birth and death were surrounded by ritual observation precisely because they were the most profound transitions over which human beings had no control. The midwife, the mourning rites, the burial customs — all acknowledged the appointed character of these transitions that frame all the other times of life.
3: A time to kill and a time to heal — the most morally complex pair in the poem. The wisdom tradition acknowledged that the same action can be appropriate or inappropriate depending on context and timing. The laws of holy war (Deut 7, 20) sanctioned killing in designated circumstances; the healing function of Levitical priests (examining skin diseases, Lev 13-14) acknowledged a time for restoration. Neither action is inherently good or evil; timing and context are the moral determinants.
4: A time to weep and a time to laugh; a time to mourn and a time to dance — the liturgical calendar of Israel organized exactly this rhythm. The fasts (Zechariah 7:3-5; Ecclesiastes 8:19) and the feast days (the three pilgrimage festivals) were the covenant community's institutionalization of the weeping-mourning and rejoicing-dancing poles. Paul captures the same wisdom in Romans 12:15: rejoice with those who rejoice; weep with those who weep. The appropriate emotional response depends on communal discernment of the present time.
5: A time to throw away stones and a time to gather stones — likely a reference to agricultural field-clearing and preparation: stones were gathered when clearing a field and thrown when rendering an enemy's field useless (2 Kings 3:19). The pairing of embrace and refrain from embracing reflects the purity codes and social customs governing physical contact in Israelite and ANE culture. The same actions are appropriate or inappropriate depending on relational and ritual context.
6: A time to seek and a time to lose; a time to keep and a time to throw away — the management of possessions in covenant life. The Jubilee law (Lev 25) institutionalized the time to throw away accumulated property; the tithe created a rhythm of keeping and giving. Holding on beyond its appointed time becomes hoarding; letting go before the right time is waste. Timing, not the action itself, is the moral category in the wisdom tradition.
7: A time to tear and a time to sew; a time to keep silence and a time to speak — tearing garments was the ritual of mourning (Genesis 37:29; 2 Samuel 3:31); sewing them up was the return to normal life. The movement between silence and speech reflects the wisdom tradition's strong emphasis on appropriate speech: Proverbs consistently warns against speaking at the wrong time (Proverbs 17:27-28). Ecclesiastes intensifies this: timing determines whether speech is wisdom or folly.
8: A time to love and a time to hate; a time for war and a time for peace — the poem's climax. The covenant theology of Israel distinguished between proper objects of love (YHWH, neighbor) and proper objects of hate (evil, injustice) rather than prescribing absolute pacifism or belligerence. The prophetic hope for a time when they shall beat their swords into plowshares (Isaiah 2:4) — a permanent time for peace — is the eschatological horizon that Qohelet's poem points toward without reaching.
9: What gain does the worker have from his toil? — the yitron question returns after the time poem. The juxtaposition is significant: even within the divinely ordered structure of appointed times (vv1-8), the question of lasting profit remains. The poem does not answer the yitron question; it reframes it within divine ordering. The context of divine appointment means the work is not random — but within the under the sun frame, the profit question remains open until the theology of gift is supplied in vv12-13.
10: I have seen the business that God has given to the children of man to be occupied with — Qohelet uses inyan (business, occupation) to describe the human vocation of toil. The same word appears in 1:13 as the unhappy business God has given. The ambivalence is deliberate: human work is God-given but burdensome, purposeful but frustrating. The theological anthropology is post-fall: work was the original human vocation (Genesis 2:15) but is now accompanied by the thorns and thistles of Genesis 3:17-19.
11: He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity (olam) in the human heart — the anthropological center of Ecclesiastes. The human heart has an intuition of transcendence — a sense of olam (eternity, the ages) — that the material world cannot satisfy. This is the inner experience that creates the frustration of under the sun existence: we are made for eternity but cannot grasp it from within the mortal frame. Augustine's 'Our heart is restless until it rests in Thee' is the Christian form of Ecclesiastes 3:11b.
12: There is nothing better for them than to rejoice and to do good throughout their lives — the positive form of the carpe diem principle. Do good (asah tov) adds the ethical dimension to mere enjoyment: wisdom-based enjoyment includes active goodness, not just passive consumption. The ANE carpe diem passages (Siduri, the Harper's Song) focus on eating and drinking; Qohelet adds the moral dimension of doing good, grounding enjoyment in the character of the God who gives it.
13: Everyone should eat and drink and take pleasure in his toil — this is God's gift — the explicit identification of ordinary enjoyment as divine gift. The Second Temple wisdom tradition developed this: Ben Sira (Sirach Ecclesiastes 14:11-16) cites the divine gift of enjoyment; Paul echoes the same: everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving (1 Timothy 4:4). The gift-frame transforms the experience: what would be escapism becomes gratitude toward the giver.
14: Whatever God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it and nothing taken from it — the permanence of divine acts contrasted with the transience of human works. Deuteronomy 29:29 reserves the secret things for YHWH while the revealed things belong to Israel. Qohelet acknowledges the same boundary: human wisdom cannot add to or subtract from what God establishes. The NT parallel: heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away (Matthew 24:35).
15: What is has already been, and what is to be already has been. God seeks out what has been driven away — divine memory preserving what human forgetfulness erases. The phrase 'God seeks out what has been driven away' may mean God retrieves what has been pushed aside in history, or that God brings back the past for accountability. Either way it establishes divine memory as the counter to human forgetfulness (Ecclesiastes 1:11): the cycles that Qohelet observes under the sun are held in the divine memory that transcends them.
16: In the place of justice, even there was wickedness — the corruption of legal institutions. The law courts were the primary venue for social justice in ancient Israel (Deuteronomy 16:18: judges and officers in all your towns); their corruption was a prophetic target (Isaiah 5:23; Amos 5:12; Micah 3:11). Qohelet observes the failure of human justice without calling for reform; the prophetic answer is the coming judge who will execute justice perfectly (Isaiah 11:3-4; Ecclesiastes 42:1-4).
17: God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every purpose and every work — the appeal to divine eschatological judgment as the resolution of the justice-gap. Qohelet affirms divine judgment without specifying when; the time for judgment is God's appointed time. The wisdom tradition maintained faith in divine judgment even when its timing was opaque (Proverbs 11:21; Ecclesiastes 24:12). Qohelet's contribution is to locate this confidence within the appointed times framework of Ecclesiastes 3:1-8.
18: God is testing them so they may see that they are but animals — the humiliation of human pretension. The testing vocabulary (lavor = to sift, test) appears in the covenant-testing of Israel in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 8:2). The comparison to animals is not a denial of human dignity but a corrective to human pretension: what makes humans different from animals (the olam in the heart, v11; the spirit going upward, v21) is not their physical constitution or mortal fate, which is shared with animals.
19: What happens to the children of man is what happens to the beasts; the same thing happens to them — the deliberate provocation that uses under the sun observation. Both humans and animals breathe the same air (ruach = breath/spirit); both die. The observation is empirically accurate. The restriction to the under the sun frame is essential: Qohelet is not denying the distinction between human spirit and animal breath; he is testing what the unaided observer can claim from within the mortal frame alone.
20: All go to one place. All are from the dust and all return to dust — the Genesis 3:19 text in application: you are dust, and to dust you shall return. Both the Mesopotamian creation myths (humans made from clay/dust) and Genesis agree on the material constitution of humanity. The dust-return is the universal human fate that Qohelet insists on even for the wise and noble, as Job insisted before him (Job 10:9; Ecclesiastes 34:15). The resurrection is the NT's answer to this verdict.
21: Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes downward? — deliberate epistemological restraint about what under the sun observation cannot determine. This is not denial of the spirit's upward movement but honest agnosticism: the observer under the sun cannot verify what happens to the spirit after death. The NT's answer: Christ's resurrection makes the upward journey visible and certain. What Qohelet leaves as 'who knows?' the resurrection of Christ answers (1 Corinthians 15:42-44).
22: There is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work, for that is his portion; for who can bring him back to see what will be after him? — the third carpe diem passage, grounded in mortality. Portion (heleq) is the covenantal allocation of what each person receives from God — used for the tribal land allotments (Numbers 18:20; Joshua 19:9) and extended to encompass one's share in life generally. Qohelet is not advocating hedonism but accepting the divinely allocated portion as sufficient — a covenantal acceptance of creatureliness.
